summary: Rare opener, tight set, epic Spanish Jam, solid second half
blurb: The show kicks off with the rare “Walkin’ The Dog” and builds into a high‑energy first set. A strong second set features “Shakedown Street,” an excellent “Estimated Prophet → Eyes Of The World” segue, and a soaring 15‑minute “Spanish Jam.” The encore “Keep Your Day Job” falls flat.
review:
On March 29, 1984 at the Marin County Veterans Auditorium the Dead delivered a compact but memorable performance. Opening with the seldom‑heard “Walkin’ The Dog,” Jerry Garcia’s colorful glasses and his jaw‑dropping lead work set an excited tone that carried through an upbeat first set of classics like “Bertha” and “Bird Song.”  The second set truly shines: “Shakedown Street” launches a groove‑filled run into a pristine “Estimated Prophet → Eyes Of The World,” followed by a thunderous drum duet and the band’s most celebrated moment – a 15‑minute, soaring “Spanish Jam” that showcases Brent’s subtle keyboards underpinned by Phil’s crisp bass and tight drumming from Mickey and Bill.  While the encore “Keep Your Day Job” never quite catches fire, the overall arc—from rare opener to epic jam—makes this benefit show a standout entry in the ’84 run.

Among the available tapes, the Walker‑Scotton SBD (gd1984-03-29.sbd.walker-scotton.miller.32515.sbeok.flac16) offers the clearest capture of the venue ambience and Jerry’s flamboyant stage presence, making it the definitive listening choice for fans who want to hear every nuance of this tight, high‑energy performance.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Two recordings are explicitly positive (highlighting the rare opener, Spanish Jam and Estimated Prophet → Eyes of the World), while two are mixed but still note solid playing. Consistent praise across multiple sources yields a moderate positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Excellent soundboard capture with clear, crisp mix; highlights the rare opener and epic Spanish Jam while preserving venue ambience.

key_highlights: Rare “Walkin’ The Dog” opener builds excitement in a small‑venue setting
"Shakedown Street" launches a high‑energy second set
15‑minute "Spanish Jam" provides the show’s climactic moment
Encore "Keep Your Day Job" is noticeably weaker

band_performance.Jerry: Jerry’s lead work was vivid and playful, with his guitar shining throughout; a brief mix dip during “Shakedown Street” left his tone thin on one board but overall his playing remained jaw‑dropping.
band_performance.Phil: Phil’s bass was clear and punchy, especially evident on the audience tape where it cut through the mix.
band_performance.Bob: Bob contributed a humorous spoken moment (“voices tell me what to take”), adding character to the set.
